stimulus: The Awakening is an 1899 novel by Kate Chopin. The novel’s protagonist, Edna Pontellier, chafes against the social roles assigned to women of her time. Although she lacks the freedom to remake her external circumstances, she insists on a private inner life that belongs only to her, as is evident when she says, ______blank
stem: Which quotation from The Awakening most effectively illustrates the claim?

answerOptions.content: “I would give up the unessential; I would give my money, I would give my life for my children; but I wouldn’t give myself.”
“The children appeared before her like antagonists who had overcome her; who had overpowered and sought to drag her into the soul’s slavery for the rest of her days.”
“I am no longer one of Mr. Pontellier’s possessions to dispose of or not. I give myself where I choose.”
“The voice of the sea is seductive; never ceasing, whispering, clamoring, murmuring, inviting the soul to wander for a spell in abysses of solitude.”

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer because it directly illustrates the claim that Edna insists on retaining a private inner life of her own. By declaring that she is no longer her husband’s “possession” and that she “gives [herself] where [she] choose[s],” Edna asserts ownership over her inner self even though she cannot wholly remake her external circumstances. The phrase “I give myself” aligns with the claim that her inner life belongs to her alone.

Choice A is incorrect because the quotation distinguishes “the unessential” from the inner self, but it is framed around her children rather than around her insistence on a private self in her social situation. Choice B is incorrect because it dramatizes the threat to Edna’s autonomy without showing her insistence on a private inner life. Choice D is incorrect because it describes the lure of the sea and solitude, not Edna’s claim to her own inner life.
